_note: The Flatline Sessions III — Mona Lisa Underdrive. Chapter spine: four PoV campaign braids (Kumiko / Slick / Mona / Angie) interleaved in story order. All prose is our own original in-world writing.
game: THE FLATLINE SESSIONS III — MONA LISA UNDERDRIVE

chapters.title: The Smoke
chapters.pov_desc: A Yakuza lord's daughter, shipped to London while her father's war burns at home.
intro: Your father sent you to London the way other men send documents to a vault: quietly, expensively, and marked for retrieval when the trouble is over. The trouble is a war inside the Yakuza, and your mother is four months dead in Tokyo, and nobody will say her name.

The man who is to keep you is called Swain, and his townhouse smells of old carpets and new obligations. In your coat pocket you carry the Maas-Neotek unit — a ghost in a slab of biosoft, your father's parting gift.
His name is Colin. He is at your shoulder before the wheels stop, visible to you alone, and he already does not trust the house you are standing in.
outro: Swain's hospitality is a fence around you, and the woman who walked through it like weather is called Sally Shears — mirrors where her eyes should be, and a way of standing that empties a room of threats.

Colin has counted the guards and the exits and the lies per hour, and the total troubles him. Your father's man is trading in something over your head, and Sally has been drafted into it against her will.

She took you to the Portobello market in the snow and bought you tea like a normal person, and told you, mirror-blank: 'Anything happens, kid, you find me.' You believe her. She is the only thing in London you believe.

chapters.title: Dog Solitude
chapters.pov_desc: Sculptor of killer machines on a poisoned plain, who loses time when he's stressed.
intro: Dog Solitude is a hundred flat miles of rust and toxin where the century dumped its mistakes, and in the middle of it stands the Factory, and in the Factory you build your machines: the Judge, the Witch, the Corpsegrinder — the trial you can't remember, made out of steel that can.
You do favors for Kid Afrika because once, in Atlantic City, the Kid did one for you. You hoped he'd forget. Men like the Kid file favors the way banks file liens.

Today his hover crosses the Solitude with a girl riding shotgun and something in the back on a stretcher: a man, unconscious, wired to a slab of gray biosoft the size of a suitcase. 'This is the Count,' says the Kid. 'Keep him plugged in, keep him breathing, keep it quiet.'
outro: Gentry came down from his loft and saw the slab and forgot to be furious: an aleph, he says. A biosoft big enough to hold an approximation of everything — a whole universe in a gray brick, and the man on the stretcher is jacked into it at the root.
Cherry checks the Count's vitals like the med-tech she almost was, and says whoever he is, somebody paid millions to park him here — or to hide him.
Out on the Solitude, weather's moving in. And on the interstate, three days out, something with money has already started asking directions to your door.

chapters.title: Florida
chapters.pov_desc: Sixteen, working Cleveland and Florida, with a face somebody else already owns.
intro: Florida is a squat over a burned-out arcade, Eddy's schemes, and the smell of the dead ocean coming through the boards. You're sixteen. You tell people eighteen. Eddy tells people whatever sells.
You've got one thing that's yours: nights with the simstim deck, riding Angie Mitchell's sensorium — HER life, HER lights, the way the whole world loves her back.

Tonight Eddy's got a suit named Prior buying the drinks, and the suit keeps looking at you like a man checking a delivery against the invoice. The job is money like Eddy's never smelled. Nobody says what the job is.
outro: Prior moved you both out of the squat by morning — real hotel, real shower, clothes with tags. Eddy's already spending money he hasn't seen, planning empires out loud to the mirror.
But you caught the look Prior gave Eddy when Eddy wasn't looking, and it was the look the wholesaler gives packaging: the goods are you, Mona. Only you.
And in the hotel simstim rig, Angie Mitchell smiled down some red carpet, and for one bad second it felt like looking into a mirror somebody else was renting out.

chapters.title: Malibu
chapters.pov_desc: The most famous face on the planet, fresh out of rehab, listening for gods gone quiet.
intro: The house in Malibu is glass and permission: Sense/Net owns it, the way Sense/Net owns your schedule, your image, and the polite fiction that you chose any of this.

You came back from the clinic clean. The designer dust is gone, and with it — the voices. The loa haven't ridden you since Jamaica; Legba's crossroads stand empty in your dreams for the first time since your father's wire went into your head.

Hilton Swift calls twice a day, network-warm. Continuity writes its endless book and watches you through every camera it owns. And somewhere under the silence where gods used to speak, something is trying very hard to reach you — and being *blocked*.
outro: Porphyre did your hair, gossiped like a court and defended you like a garrison, and told you plainly the network's been *managing* your rehab like a product relaunch. Which it is.

Continuity answered your questions about the vodou constructs with citations, and about the dust with evasions, and about your dreams with a silence that lasted 1.6 seconds too long. Machines don't hesitate. Unless something makes them.

You told Swift you'd fly east and shoot the Sprawl segments. What you didn't tell him: the dreams are coming back — factory dark, a gray box, a boy's voice saying your name — and this time you're going toward them.

chapters.title: The Oracle of Lost Technology
chapters.pov_desc: Learning what her keeper trades in — and who Sally Shears used to be.
intro: Sally has business in the Sprawl, and takes you along the way she does everything — abruptly, competently, without asking Swain. It is the first time since Tokyo that anyone has treated you as company instead of cargo.

There is an alley off a market street where a dead man still does business: the Finn, or what he had himself encoded into, a construct in an armored shrine that talks like the smell of frying and old wiring.
Sally calls it visiting an oracle. Colin, in your ear, calls it the most interesting felony he has ever witnessed.
outro: The Finn knew Sally by an older name — Molly, out of Chiba, razors under her nails and a dead cowboy in her past — and he read her trouble like a bar tab: somebody up the well is squeezing her, through Swain, with data no living person should have.

3Jane, the Finn said, and even the alley seemed to get colder. Last heir of the family whose empire once ran half of high orbit, dead now — dead in the flesh, and vindictive in whatever she paid to become instead.
'Kid,' Sally told you, walking back through the snow, 'whatever happens in the next few weeks — none of it's going to be what it looks like.' Colin agrees. That is what frightens you.

chapters.title: The Work
chapters.pov_desc: In a Baltimore clinic, being sculpted into somebody else's mirror.
intro: Baltimore. A clinic with no sign and a surgeon named Gerald whose hands smell of soap and whose eyes don't ask questions because he's paid not to.

Prior has stopped pretending you're a person and started handling you like freight with lungs. Eddy signed papers he didn't read and got taken 'to see about the money.' You've stopped believing you'll see Eddy again.
On the table next to the consent forms somebody left a fan magazine, and Angie Mitchell smiles up off the cover at the girl who's about to be worn like her coat.
outro: The bandages are off. In the mirror: cheekbones that aren't yours, a jaw that isn't yours, HER face lying over your own like ice on a river — Mona underneath, moving in the dark.
Gerald was gentle, at the end, in a way that scared you worse than Prior: 'Whatever they've hired you for, girl, be done with it fast, and be somewhere else when it finishes.'

You still dream your own face. You hold onto that. Wiz makes it easier and worse. Somewhere north, the woman you're wearing has started traveling east, and the two of you are already falling toward the same address.

chapters.title: The Aleph
chapters.pov_desc: Keeper of a comatose cowboy and a gray box with a universe inside.
intro: The Count's been on your bench for a week, breathing on schedule, dreaming into the gray slab. Cherry's rigged monitors out of scrap; Gentry hasn't slept — the aleph has eaten his whole cosmology and started on his manners.
Gentry says it isn't a databank. It's an *approximation of everything* — the matrix folded into a brick, with something living in the folds.

Tonight Gentry means to jack in through the Count's tap and read the shape of God off the inside of a stolen box. You've been elected to hold his line. You lose time when you're stressed, Slick. Try not to be stressed.
outro: Inside the aleph there's a gray house, and in the gray house there's a boy who used to be the hottest cowboy in the Sprawl. Bobby. The Count. He talked to you like a man renovating a universe: 'Tell whoever's coming — it's MINE now. We're almost done.'

There's a woman in there with him sometimes, Gentry says, at the edges — a dead woman from up the well, watching everything with proprietary eyes. 3Jane. Even her name drops the Factory's temperature.

And Cherry came up from the stretcher pale: the Count's vitals dip every time the aleph works. Whatever's being finished in there, it's running on the last of him. On the interstate, the hired convoy is a day out now, and it's driving toward your box.

chapters.title: The Loa's Price
chapters.pov_desc: Flying east through the ruins of her own head, asking gods why they went quiet.
intro: The Sense/Net jet eats the continent while Piper Hill runs your call sheet and pretends this trip is about production values. You've started palming the new dust the network doctor keeps 'adjusting' — the designer calm that came with your rehab, that tastes faintly of somebody else's agenda.

Off the dust, the dreams sharpen nightly: the factory dark, the gray box, the boy. And under them, like a pulse under floorboards, the sense of the loa trying to reach you through a wall somebody built inside your own lace.
Tonight, over the middle of America, you're going to stop being managed. You're going to jack the lace raw and knock on the silence yourself.
outro: She came through where Legba couldn't: Mamman Brigitte, mistress of the dead, walking the ruins of your channel like a widow inspecting fire damage.

The truth, in her voice: the dust was never medicine. It was a LOCK — bought and paid for, cutting you off from the matrix's new weather while other hands moved. The buyer: a dead woman up the well who hates what you're becoming. 3Jane.

And the boy in the gray box is real, and the box is real, and the wedding the loa spoke of when you were fifteen is REAL, and it is days away, and the venue is a factory in a poisoned nowhere that you have been dreaming in perfect detail. You told Piper to change the flight plan. East, then north. You're done being the network's dream. Time to go have your own.

chapters.title: Underground
chapters.pov_desc: Alone in London with a ghost for a guide, and a keeper turned kidnapper's clerk.
intro: Sally is gone — pulled north across the Atlantic by the people squeezing her — and Swain's house has stopped pretending to be a home. Through a heating grate, in your slippers, you heard the accounting: Swain is brokering the abduction of the most famous woman in the world, payment in wholesale data, on the instruction of something that used to be a person called 3Jane.
Colin has finished being charming. 'We are leaving,' he says, 'now, before the house decides you heard that.'
There is a man in Brixton called Tick who runs the matrix for Sally and owes her everything. Colin knows the way. The Underground runs all night. Walk, don't run, Miss Yanaka.
outro: Tick's flat smells of solder and takeaway, and his rig showed you the thing itself: the aleph, hanging in the matrix like a gray moon, and the whole grid leaning toward it the way a field leans toward weather.

From Tick's line you reached Sally, one burst, compressed: SWAIN SELLS YOU. 3JANE BUYS. THE GIRL IS BAIT AND SO ARE YOU. She read it twice and said, mirrors steady: 'Good work, kid. Now stay with Tick and watch what happens to people who squeeze me.'
Colin, impossibly, is smiling. 'Your father sent you to London to keep you out of a war,' he says. 'On current evidence, he mis-shipped.'

chapters.title: The Switch
chapters.pov_desc: Wearing a stolen face to a kidnapping, in the wrong place at exactly the planned time.
intro: The New Suzuki Envoy is the kind of hotel that has weather of its own. You've been walked through it twice like a rehearsal nobody explains, in clothes chosen to photograph as somebody else's.

Prior's crew talks over you in fragments: the mark arrives tonight; the switch happens in the underground garage; the double — that's you, Mona — stays with the car. Nobody says the mark's name. Nobody has to. You're wearing her face.
Wiz keeps the edges bright. Under the wiz, cold and clear as Cleveland rain, one thought: Lanette always said, when the job smells like this, be the first one to stop following the plan.
outro: It came apart in the garage in eleven seconds: the mirror-eyed woman cutting through Prior's crew like a rumor of surgery, Angie Mitchell — the real one, YOUR face on her bones — calm as church in the middle of it, and Prior on the concrete not getting up.
The mirror woman looked at you, then at Angie, then did the arithmetic 3Jane paid for — and broke it. 'Plans change,' she said. 'Both of you. In the car. NOW.'

You're on a northbound freight lift-out with a kidnapper who won't kidnap, a superstar who keeps looking at your face like an apology, and nobody left alive who knows which of you is which. North: a factory, a wedding, a gray moon you've started dreaming about too.

chapters.title: The Siege of Dog Solitude
chapters.pov_desc: Defending the Factory with art: the Judge, the Witch, and everything that cuts.
intro: They came onto the Solitude at dawn: hovers, hired muscle, and a command van that never breaks radio silence — 3Jane's money, come to collect the aleph and burn the receipts, which is everyone you know.

Then out of the north, unannounced, a second party: a woman with mirrors for eyes driving like the laws of physics owe her money, carrying two girls with one face — and the face is the one on every screen in the world.

Gentry's wiring the Factory's defenses into his loft rig. Cherry's got the Count stabilized in the shaking. And you, Slick Henry, are going out into the yard to introduce the visitors to your art. The Judge has been waiting its whole life for this.
outro: The yard is a museum of the afternoon: burned hovers, the Judge dripping hydraulic fluid and glory, the Witch half-slagged and still twitching toward the enemy she was made for. They did not take the Factory.

The mirror-eyed woman fought like the Judge's little sister and vouched for the two girls with one face. The famous one walked straight to the Count's stretcher like she'd dreamed the route — because she had.

Now Gentry says the aleph is spinning up on its own, drawing power the Factory doesn't have to give. Something in there is getting married or getting born, and everyone on this poisoned plain has been invited whether they RSVP'd or not.

chapters.title: Mona Lisa Underdrive
chapters.pov_desc: At the gray box at the end of every dream, where all the roads were always going.
intro: The Factory at night, after the battle: torchlight, machine shadows, a boy on a stretcher with a universe plugged into his skull — and every dream you've had since Jamaica standing around you in the dark, keeping its appointment.

Mamman Brigitte walks the edges of your lace. The loa crowd the wire like wedding guests. 3Jane coils inside the aleph, spiteful and dead and rich enough to matter anyway — the last obstacle between you and the boy who's been calling.

His name is Bobby Newmark. The Count. You met him once, in a nightclub under siege, when you were both sixteen and the gods were new. He's been building a place for you inside everything. All you have to do is die into it. All you have to do is choose.
outro: You chose. The lace opened like a door at last, and the meat you left on the stretcher stopped, gently, while the loa sang you across — and Bobby was there at the crossroads, real as rain, in the bright forever of the aleph. What the gods called a wedding. What the tabloids will call a tragedy. What it actually is: the two of you, home.

Outside: Molly walked away into the snow with nothing to squeeze her ever again — the Finn's construct saw to 3Jane's estate. Kumiko testified, politely, and Swain's operation went down with all hands. Slick buried the Judge's enemies and kept the Factory. Cherry stayed. Gentry finally saw the Shape, and it was two kids holding hands.

And a girl from Cleveland with a borrowed face stepped off a lift-out into a Sense/Net contract, screen-tested by destiny itself: the network needed an Angie, and there she was — Mona, overdrive, the future's new smile. Somewhere in the aleph, the original approves. THE END — and the trilogy with it. Thanks for playing, cowboy.
